title: Analysis: UK sales of electric vehicles just overtook petrol cars for the first time - Carbon Brief
gist: UK battery EV sales have overtaken petrol car sales for the first time on a 12-month rolling basis (516,490 BEVs vs 504,010 petrol cars in the 12 months to May 2026), even as the car industry lobbies to water down the ZEV mandate. EV sales grew 34% year-on-year in May 2026 while petrol car sales fell 14%. Globally, EV sales grew 20% in 2025 to one in four new cars sold, with IEA projecting another 15% growth in 2026.

themes.theme: UK EV inflection point — sales parity crossed

insights.claim: UK battery electric vehicle sales exceeded petrol car sales for the first time on a rolling 12-month basis in the period to May 2026, with 516,490 BEVs sold against 504,010 petrol cars.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A structural demand inflection has occurred in the UK market — this milestone undermines the car industry's argument that consumer demand is insufficient to meet ZEV mandate targets.
insights.quote: In the 12 months to May 2026, UK consumers bought 516,490 new BEVs, against only 504,010 new petrol cars.

insights.claim: UK BEV sales grew 34% year-on-year to 43,931 units in May 2026, while petrol car sales fell 14% to 35,068 units in the same month.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The monthly momentum strongly supports continued market share gains — the divergence in growth rates (EV +34%, petrol -14%) implies the gap will widen materially within 12-18 months.
insights.quote: EV sales grew 34% to reach 43,931, while petrol cars were down 14% to 35,068.

insights.claim: The UK car industry argues demand for EVs is insufficient to meet ZEV mandate targets, despite having 'over-complied' with the mandate to date and EV sales now exceeding petrol cars.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Industry lobbying to weaken the ZEV mandate contradicts actual sales data — investors should treat mandate rollback rhetoric as a regulatory risk overlay rather than a demand signal.
insights.quote: The car industry argues that demand for these cars is too low to meet the requirements of the ZEV mandate, despite the fact that the industry has 'over-complied' to date.

insights.claim: Hybrids are the most popular type of new car in the UK with 56,321 units sold in May 2026, but hybrid sales growth is near-flat at just 2% year-on-year, while plug-in hybrids grew 24% to 22,167 units.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Hybrid stagnation signals these vehicles are a transitional technology, not a destination — OEMs over-invested in traditional hybrids face stranded product risk as BEV adoption accelerates.
insights.quote: Some 56,321 hybrids were sold in May 2026...This is an increase of 1,181 year-on-year, or just 2%.

insights.claim: Global EV sales grew 20% in 2025, reaching one in every four new cars sold worldwide, with the IEA projecting a further 15% growth in 2026.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: At 25% global market share and 20% annual growth, EVs are no longer a niche — battery supply chain, charging infrastructure and grid capacity are now strategic investment priorities.
insights.quote: Globally, EV sales grew by 20% in 2025 and accounted for one in every four new cars sold, according to the International Energy Agency (IEA). The agency said that global EV sales were set to grow by another 15% in 2026.

insights.claim: The EU market saw more BEVs sold than petrol cars for the first time in a single month in December 2025, a milestone the UK first achieved in December 2022 — indicating the EU is following the UK's trajectory with roughly a 3-year lag.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The EU monthly milestone suggests the broader European market is approaching the same structural inflection — benefiting EV manufacturers and suppliers with EU exposure.
insights.quote: The new analysis for the UK follows a similar milestone for the EU, with more BEVs having been sold in the month of December 2025 than petrol cars.

insights.claim: The UK ZEV mandate sets rising annual targets for the share of new car sales that must be zero-emissions vehicles, primarily battery EVs, and the car industry and some unions are actively pushing to water it down.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Political pressure to weaken the mandate creates policy uncertainty even as market demand independently supports EV growth — mandate dilution would primarily benefit legacy ICE manufacturers by reducing compliance costs.
insights.quote: The news comes amid a battle over the future of the UK's 'zero-emissions vehicle' (ZEV) mandate, which the car industry and some unions are pushing to water down.
